text: going. God, we made mistakes, but did we really deserve all this ? Been three days since they burned the bodies. It’s mostly been quiet, though Mandy told me there’ve been a few brawls amongst the other people left in town. Lots of tension in the air. I went down to the bar on 19 th to see for myself. They still got booze. A week ago I would’ve said that was the only reason a place like that could be so busy, but I think that’s wrong now. I got the sense that there were so many folks there because everyone’s scared and nobody wants to be alone right now . This town took enough of a hit when the armies rolled through the first time. We weathered the worst of it. Hell, who the fuck would bother targeting Wyoming, apart from the oil? We’ve maybe had it too easy. Sounds like it’s gone to shit everywhere else. Every now and then someone drifts up from Colorado or wanders in along I-80 from further west, and none of them have good stories to tell. They say Denver’s just a radioactive crater . Now we’ve got these robot sons of bitches coming in and telling us what parts of town we can’t go into anymore. How the hell’s that acceptable? I bet some of them were even part of the group that came through during the war. For the most part, they left the city alone. Most of the fighting was at the old Air Force Base – we still see smoke from there, sometimes, on the clear days – but there was still damage done . They were talking down at the bar like we’re all going to get together and do something about this. Gather weapons and force them out. Sure, there are bots that aren’t causing any trouble, but nobody seems to care about them now. They’re all a problem, it was them that fought in the war, them that was killing women and children . All those idiots already forgot it was us that started dropping the bombs . I’m getting myself all worked up. Got to stop. I’m likely to do something stupid when I get this way . Ronin stopped, placed his finger between the pages, and closed the book around it. Was there any analysis he could perform on what he’d just read that wouldn’t come away confused ? Humans skewed their perception of events based on their emotions. He knew that firsthand, thanks largely to recent experiences. The journal’s writer even alluded to it in his entry, perhaps realizing it after he looked up to see the steady degradation of his handwriting, the increasing pressure with each pen- stroke . Ronin entered the hallway and walked to the attic hatch. A first glance rarely revealed everything. Reaching up, he grasped the string and opened the hatch. The ladder protested his ascent, and the floorboards creaked despite his slow, careful steps. Standing by the window, he was directly above Lara’s room . What would she say when he told her what the journal contained? I knew it , or doesn’t surprise me , or perhaps you fucking buckets-of-bolts bastards ? Or, would her lips turn down and her eyes glisten with sorrow he couldn’t comprehend, sorrow for the memory of long-dead people she’d never known? For people who were taken from their homes — from this house — and killed ? He stopped at the window and stared out, taking in the scene. The park was mostly dark, save for the light reflected on the pond; it was a patch of black between the gentle glow of streetlamps on either side. A view that must have been familiar to the man who wrote the journal . Ronin ran his optics over the blankets and magazines on the floor, the jars of food in the bookcase, the table with its single chair. What would it have been like to be in the place of the writer ? He reached through his memory, accessing data from his many forays into, around, and beyond the Dust. Almost every night had been spent alone, and he’d never given that fact any thought. He’d encountered plenty of people out there, leaving most dead or deactivated in the dirt . The Dust favored the quick and the ruthless. There was no camaraderie to be found out there . Yet hadn’t he encountered, time and again, bots and humans alike who ran in groups? A pair of synths, half a dozen humans, all eager for supplies, for food, for the imminent, violent confrontation with Ronin that was likely their only social interaction outside their small group ? Somehow, that thought chain brought him to a new realization — it was possible that he didn’t have to go out alone. Lara was a survivor. Like any human, she had her weaknesses, but there was strength in her beyond his ability to classify and measure. What would it be like to walk the Dust with her? To converse with her over the long days and nights, to have an extra pair of eyes watching his back ? To have someone to watch out for other than himself . No. The Dust was no place for her. No place for anyone . The dangers to him were too great as it was, and he couldn’t consider putting her at such risk for even a moment . He opened the journal again, and was still reading when the rising sun crested the roof enough to cast its pure light on the treetops across the street . They’re coming for me , the last page began, the letters distorted by haste. I think everyone else is already gone, and in a few more days, they’ll have that wall finished and I’ll be trapped. I don’t know why I stayed this long. Probably because I can still remember the smiles on the faces of my wife
